mnemonics: SU = Switch User. Imagine a superhero switching identities to gain new powers (privileges).
keyPoints: The 'su' command is used to switch to another user account, most commonly the superuser (root).
Using 'su' alone defaults to switching to the root user, while 'su username' switches to the specified user.
The command requires the password of the target user account, not the current user's password, to authenticate the switch.
commonMistakes: Confusing 'su' (substitute user) with 'sudo' (substitute user do), which executes a single command with elevated privileges.
Assuming 'su' requires the password of the current user instead of the password of the target user.
Forgetting that using 'su' without the '-' argument does not load the target user's full environment (e.g., .profile, .bashrc).
